A response/letter with no abstract or methodological content.
Focuses on dual-energy tomosynthesis of the proximal femur, not DSA/fluoroscopic angiographic sequences.
CNN-based stent segmentation in digital fluoroscopy images with quantitative accuracy results.
Deep learning method for predicting DSA/fluoroscopy working angles in aneurysm treatment with quantitative evaluation against expert ground truth.
An editorial with no abstract or original computational contribution.
Appears to be a commentary/opinion piece with no abstract or methodological detail.
Deep learning system for automated TICI scoring from DSA sequences with quantitative agreement metrics.
Deep learning pose estimation/tracking network for aortic stents in DSA/fluoroscopy with detailed methodology and quantitative metrics.
Geometric deep learning method for catheter segmentation in cerebral angiography fluoroscopy with quantitative Dice and tip-distance results.
Title indicates a deep learning model for stroke subtype identification based on DSA imaging, meeting modality and technical criteria.
3D CNN-based classification of coronary angiogram video quality using temporal DSA sequences with quantitative performance metrics.
Brain MRI segmentation study unrelated to DSA/fluoroscopic angiography.
Uses fundus fluorescein angiography (ophthalmic), not DSA or fluoroscopic imaging.
A COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy study unrelated to angiographic imaging.
Deep learning marker detection in fluoroscopy for C-arm pose estimation with quantitative accuracy results.
A letter response with no abstract or original computational methodology.
Uses a static pair of fluoroscopic radiographs rather than temporally ordered image sequences.
Coronary angiography-based deep learning stenosis detection with quantitative performance metrics.
Appears to be an editorial/commentary piece on AI in coronary angiography without original computational methodology.
Uses fluorescein angiography fundus images for classification, not DSA/fluoroscopic temporally ordered sequences.
Deep learning 3D U-Net detection/segmentation of cerebral aneurysms on DSA images with quantitative sensitivity results.
Deep learning coronary artery segmentation on angiography image sequences to quantify distensibility over the cardiac cycle, with quantitative results.
Fluoroscopy-based catheter shape reconstruction combined with sensor fusion, a computational image analysis task on fluoroscopic sequences.
Editor's choice commentary summarizing other articles, not an original computational study.
Multi-modal fundus/OCT imaging for AMD lesion detection, not DSA or fluoroscopy.
CNN/U-Net based coronary stenosis segmentation on X-ray angiography with quantitative Dice/Jaccard metrics.
Uses CT coronary angiography, not DSA or fluoroscopy.
Static X-ray skeletal landmark localization for C-arm control, not temporally ordered angiographic sequences.
Uses ultra-widefield fundus photographs, not DSA/fluoroscopy imaging.
Uses dynamic CT angiography (CTA) of cerebral vessels, not DSA or fluoroscopy.
Titled as a commentary ('helps us decide') with no abstract or evidence of original computational methodology.
Deep learning model for real-time catheter tracking from fluoroscopy, a clear temporal fluoroscopic image analysis task.
Relies on coronary CT angiography (CCTA), not DSA or fluoroscopy.
Uses CT pulmonary angiography (CTPA) static/volumetric scans, not DSA or fluoroscopy sequences.
Analysis is based on stress echocardiography (ultrasound), not DSA/fluoroscopy.
Unrelated to angiographic imaging; concerns sentiment analysis text/image classification models.
Machine learning and neural network segmentation applied to angiographic images to predict cerebral aneurysm treatment outcomes, with quantitative accuracy/F1 results.

finalDecisions.rationale: The title indicates the imaging modality is indocyanine green (ICG) near-infrared fluorescence imaging used during robotic surgery to assess ureter perfusion, not DSA or X-ray fluoroscopy. Despite the word "fluoroscopy" appearing, this is a fluorescence-guided surgical video technique, which is a fundamentally different imaging modality than the angiographic DSA/fluoroscopic X-ray sequences targeted by I1. This fails I1 (and falls under E1, as it is a non-DSA/fluoroscopy modality). Additionally, no abstract is available, leaving no way to confirm methodological detail (I3) or quantitative evaluation (I4), reinforcing exclusion under E4. Reviewer B's rationale is correct; Reviewer A's inference that "fluoroscopy" in the title implies DSA/X-ray fluoroscopy is a misreading of the modality.

Reviewer B has the stronger reading of the criteria. Although the study uses machine-learning-based segmentation/detection on fluoroscopic images with quantitative results, it does not involve DSA angiography or temporally ordered image sequences (I1 fails, E2 applies): the method works from "as little as two fluoroscopic images" (pairs from different gantry angles for stereo/3D triangulation), not a temporal sequence of frames tracking contrast flow or vessel dynamics. The application domain is orthopaedic guidewire/implant placement, not angiographic (vascular) imaging analysis, so it is not relevant to a systematic review of AI methods for DSA/fluoroscopic angiographic sequences. This is a case of static/multi-view (not temporal) fluoroscopy in an unrelated clinical domain, which falls under exclusion criterion E2 (no temporally ordered sequence) and arguably E1 (not angiographic imaging).

FluoroSAM is a general-purpose language-promptable segmentation foundation model trained on 3M synthetic X-ray images spanning many anatomies and X-ray subtypes (diagnostic chest X-ray to interventional fluoroscopy), not specifically on DSA/angiographic sequences. Critically, the model operates on individual X-ray images for organ/tool segmentation and there is no indication it processes temporally ordered image sequences (a defining feature of angiographic imaging per I1, and explicitly excluded by E2 if only static images are analyzed). While it is evaluated quantitatively and has sufficient methodological detail (satisfying I3/I4), it does not meet I1/I2 in the angiography-specific sense the review requires, and is more accurately characterized as a general X-ray segmentation tool rather than a study of angiographic image sequence analysis. Reviewer B's rationale is more consistent with the criteria: the work is not specific to angiographic DSA/fluoroscopy sequences, and Reviewer A's inclusion overlooks the lack of temporal sequence analysis central to I1/E2.

The study uses WEKA/Random Forest segmentation on "pre- and postoperative DSA image pairs" - i.e., single representative static images captured before and after treatment, compared at two discrete timepoints rather than analyzing the temporally ordered frame sequence within a DSA run (contrast bolus propagation). This is fundamentally a static image comparison task (segmentation and pixel-count comparison between two snapshots), not temporal sequence analysis. This matches exclusion criterion E2 (analyzes only static images, no temporally ordered sequence), despite otherwise satisfying I2-I4 (ML segmentation task, described methodology, quantitative p-value results). Reviewer A's inclusion rationale overlooks that "pre vs post" is not the same as a temporal DSA sequence (which would involve multiple frames per acquisition capturing contrast flow dynamics). Reviewer B's exclusion rationale is correct on this point.

Although I1 is technically satisfied in the loosest sense (fluoroscopy, temporally ordered sequences) and I2-I4 are met with quantitative ConvLSTM tracking metrics, the study is about markerless lung tumor tracking for SBRT radiotherapy using an X-CAT phantom, not angiographic (DSA/vascular contrast) imaging analysis. The systematic review's scope is AI methods applied to angiographic image sequences specifically (vessel/contrast-based imaging tasks), not fluoroscopy in general for arbitrary anatomical targets. Reviewer A correctly identifies that this is domain-mismatched: the imaging target is a lung tumor tracked via fluoroscopic projection, with no vascular/angiographic content, no contrast injection, and no angiography-relevant task (vessel segmentation, DSA denoising, etc.). Reviewer B's rationale focuses only on the technical similarity (ConvLSTM, sequential frames, quantitative metrics) but ignores that the clinical/imaging domain is entirely outside angiography, which is the core scope-defining criterion for this SLR. This falls under E1 in spirit (non-angiographic imaging application) even though the modality label is fluoroscopy — the review's intent is angiographic imaging, and lung tumor tracking is a different clinical use case unrelated to vascular structures.

Although the study uses kV X-ray fluoroscopic image sequences (temporally ordered), fluoroscopy alone does not equal angiographic imaging (DSA/fluoroscopic sequences used for vascular imaging). This paper concerns markerless lung tumor tracking during radiotherapy using CNN-based segmentation on fluoroscopic/DRR images - it has nothing to do with angiographic (vascular) image analysis, which is the subject domain of this systematic review. Reviewer B's rationale focuses only on the modality (fluoroscopy) and technical/quantitative criteria (I2-I4), but overlooks that the review's scope is specifically angiographic imaging applications (vessel segmentation, DSA analysis, etc.), not general fluoroscopic imaging for tumor tracking. Reviewer A correctly identifies this domain mismatch. While I1 technically allows "fluoroscopy," the intent of the SR is angiographic/vascular imaging, and this paper is about oncology/radiotherapy tumor tracking, entirely unrelated to angiographic image analysis tasks (I2 e.g., vessel segmentation, DSA denoising). Thus it falls outside the intended scope and should be excluded.

With no abstract available, only the title can be assessed. While the title suggests DSA imaging and a deep-learning approach (satisfying I1/I2 at a surface level), there is no way to confirm I3 (sufficient technical/methodological detail to identify the principal computational approach) or I4 (quantitative evaluation results reported), and no way to rule out E2 (whether the study uses temporally ordered sequences vs. static/summary DSA frames) or E4 (whether this is merely an abstract/poster without full methodological detail). The stated protocol explicitly instructs defaulting to EXCLUDE when there is truly insufficient information to determine relevance from title alone. Reviewer A's rationale correctly applies this default; Reviewer B's INCLUDE relies on inference from the title that cannot be verified against the required criteria.
